summary: The Gemara debates whether one priest can do both the slaughter and the blood-sprinkling of the tamid, and whether the slaughterer or the sprinkler collects the blood. Multiple proofs from baraitot and mishnayot resolve that the sprinkler is the collector. The page then analyzes Ben Azzai and Rabbi Akiva's dispute about the order for carrying the tamid's limbs to the altar, with Rava explaining that all tannaim agree the head goes up first.

glossary.en: The priest who slaughters the tamid offering.
The priest who sprinkles the blood on the altar.
The priest who receives (collects) the blood in a vessel after slaughter.

rashiTranslations.en: Can one priest do both the slaughter and the blood-receiving?
Baraita: R. Yehuda says the incense and coal-pan are combined - proves single lottery is possible.
Rejection: coal-pan and incense are different because they must be done together.
Alternative reading of the baraita - incense specifically requires a new priest each time.
Explicit baraita: R. Chiyya - no separate lottery for each individual service task.
Mishna question: who sprinkles the blood? And who collects it beforehand?
Possible inference: the sprinkler also collects, since continuity of the blood service matters.
Proof from another mishna: the sprinkler and collector are the same person.
Argument from multiplicity: if the slaughterer also collected, there would be too many priests.
Rav Acha son of Rava brings further support from the Tamid mishna.
Ben Azzai's alternative limb order before Rabbi Akiva.
Rabbi Akiva: limbs brought up in order of cutting, starting with the head.
R. Yosei HaGelili's view: every good piece (head) goes up first.
Rava: both the mishna and Ben Azzai agree the head goes up first - they differ only on what follows.
Why does the right leg go up alongside the head?
Gemara asks: does every tanna agree that every limb is brought up by a single priest?
Implying one of the other winners also does it.
Because sprinkling is so beloved as an altar service.
One kohen does not collect all the blood.
As stated later (48a) one must collect all the blood.
As it says (Lev. 4) 'and all the blood.'
And it happens that a non-kohen slaughters.
Slaughter is valid by a non-kohen but collection is not.
As it says (Lev. 1) 'and he shall slaughter the bull.'
'And the kohanim shall present the blood' - from collection onward is a priestly duty.
From collection onward it is a priestly obligation, teaching slaughter is valid by a non-kohen.
Ben Katin was the KG.
He had twelve spigots which he poured water into.
There are thirteen, since thirteen services are counted in our Mishna.
But slaughter does not require sanctification of hands and feet.
Even when performed by a kohen, since it is valid by a non-kohen.
And if you say the slaughterer also receives the blood.
It turns out he needs to sanctify hands and feet for the collection.
Since collection requires it.
He would need to sanctify for the blood collection.
We too have learned this in the Mishna of Tamid: 'he came to sprinkle.'
The correct reading in Tamid is 'and he came to sprinkle.'
The order follows the path of the head - the leading parts come first.
The head and the right leg - everyone agrees they come first, derived from a verse below.
The order follows the flaying - from the leg side to the head side.
The order follows the dissection - the order of cutting after flaying.
Limb after limb, and the dissection order is specified in Tamid.
Arranged here as it is there, how is the dissection order? etc.
When he dissects, each limb is given to the winner and all stand.
Until all have received their limb, then they all proceed together.
They carry their limbs to the ramp, one after another.
The order of carrying is disputed among these tannaim.
One says one way and the other says the other.
The correct reading is: how is the dissection order? Head and right leg, two forelegs, chest and throat, two flanks, tail and left leg.
One says this and one says that.
The correct text: how is the order of excellence? Head and right leg.
Chest and throat, two flanks, tail and left leg, two forelegs.
But it is written 'the best portion - the thigh and shoulder.'
In lean animals the thigh is better since it is stronger.
The tanna of our Mishna does not rule like either of them.
This tanna follows the greatness of the limb.
One tanna follows the excellence of size, the other follows the excellence of fat.
Another verse: 'the pieces, the head, and the fat.'
What purpose does this verse serve? We have the pieces encompassing them.
Summary: one tanna follows limb significance, R. Yosi follows fat content.
Another verse: 'the pieces, the head, and the fat' - cited for the fat.
Purpose of the additional verse about the fat.

display.whats: Can one priest perform both the slaughter and the blood-sprinkling of the tamid? Multiple baraitot are cited but the question remains open.
display.title: Grouped lottery, not merged roles: why the coal-pan precedent cannot settle slaughter and sprinkling
display.hint: R. Chiyya's baraita ultimately settles that no separate lottery is held for each individual task.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does a baraita about combining the coal-pan and incense roles fail to settle whether slaughter and blood-sprinkling can also go to the same priest?
learning.coreTension: Any precedent for combining two Temple tasks under one lottery winner looks like it should generalize to other task pairs, yet the Gemara refuses to extend the coal-and-incense precedent to slaughter and sprinkling.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara distinguishes physically inseparable acts, the coal-pan and incense must happen as one continuous motion by one person, from sequential but distinct acts like slaughter and blood-sprinkling. R. Chiyya's explicit ruling that no per-task lottery is held then settles the structural question: tasks are grouped together in a single lottery round, but grouping the lottery is not the same as proving one priest performs every task in the group.
learning.resolution: No separate lottery is held for each individual task; related tasks are grouped together in one lottery round. Whether slaughter and blood-collection specifically fall to the same priest is a separate question, taken up immediately afterward.

takeaway.text: A shared lottery round does not by itself prove that the same priest performs every task within it. Physically inseparable tasks must be combined; which priest performs each sequential task is determined by separate criteria.

learning.ahaMoment: The coal-pan and incense are inseparable not just in practice but as a single combined service; that distinction between intrinsically linked services and separable ones, not the lottery's grouping, is what drives the legal analysis.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may read 'no per-task lottery' as meaning one priest does everything assigned in that lottery round. The grouping is a procedural fact about how the lottery is conducted; which specific priest performs which task within the group is answered separately, and the Gemara goes on to establish that the blood collector is the sprinkler, not the slaughterer.
learning.memoryAnchor: Coal-pan and incense are like one glove, you cannot hold the coal without the incense going in, so they must be one role. Slaughter and blood-sprinkling are two separate steps; grouped lottery, not automatically merged roles.

argumentFlow.label: Slaughter + sprinkling: one lottery?
argumentFlow.text: Did the second lottery assign slaughter and blood-sprinkling to the same priest, or separately?

argumentFlow.label: R. Yehuda baraita: coal + incense combined
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda: the lottery assigned both coal-pan carrying and incense-burning to the same priest, suggesting tasks can be combined.

argumentFlow.label: Coal and incense are inseparable
argumentFlow.text: Rejected: coal-pan and incense must be done together by one person - they are not two tasks but one combined act. The analogy fails.

argumentFlow.label: Alternative: incense must be new
argumentFlow.text: Others read the baraita as: specifically because incense must always be a new priest, the lottery needed to specify this unique restriction.

argumentFlow.label: R. Chiyya: no per-task lottery
argumentFlow.text: R. Chiyya explicitly: no separate lottery is held for each individual service. The second lottery assigned groups of related tasks.

halachic: ktores (incense) always to a new priest
coal-pan and incense as inseparable unit
narrative: R. Yehuda vs alternative reading of same baraita

misconceptions.misconception: The coal-pan and incense require two separate lottery slots.
misconceptions.correction: They are a single inseparable act, physically requiring one continuous motion, and are assigned to one priest.

misconceptions.misconception: The incense lottery worked the same as all other task lotteries.
misconceptions.correction: Incense always went to a new priest (a first-timer rule), making its lottery treatment unique among the grouped tasks.

visualizableElements.label: Coal + incense (inseparable) vs slaughter + sprinkling (separable)
visualizableElements.description: Visual showing why some task pairs are inherently linked while others are distinct

visualizableElements.label: Lottery assignment method
visualizableElements.description: How groups of related tasks were assigned in one lottery round

quizSeeds.question: Why can't the coal-pan/incense analogy prove that slaughter and blood-sprinkling can go to one priest?
quizSeeds.answer: Coal-pan and incense are physically inseparable - one person must do both simultaneously. Slaughter and blood-sprinkling are distinct sequential acts.

quizSeeds.question: What does R. Chiyya's baraita establish about how the lottery worked?
quizSeeds.answer: No separate lottery is held for each individual service task - tasks were grouped and assigned together.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Chiyya's baraita establishes that no per-task lottery was held; the question of who collected the blood is addressed in the next sugya.

display.whats: Who collects the tamid's blood - the sprinkler or the slaughterer? The Gemara proves it must be the same priest who sprinkles.
display.title: Filling the mishna's silence: why the blood collector must also be the sprinkler
display.hint: Continuity of blood service from collection through sprinkling matters: one priest should handle the blood throughout.

learning.learnerQuestion: If the mishna only names who sprinkles the blood, why does the Gemara insist the very same priest must have collected it too?
learning.coreTension: The mishna's silence about who collects the blood could just as easily mean the slaughterer, already standing at the animal, collects it; instead the Gemara reads that silence as assuming a stricter single-handler rule.
learning.coreMove: Multiple independent proofs, the priest count implied by the Tamid mishna, a direct baraita, and Rav Acha son of Rava's additional source, all converge on the same answer: continuity of custody. Blood that changes hands between collection and sprinkling carries the same invalidation risk the Gemara guards against in other blood services, so the mishna's silence is read as presupposing the sprinkler collects, not as leaving the question open.
learning.resolution: The priest who sprinkles the tamid's blood on the altar is the same priest who collected it after slaughter; continuity of custody from collection through sprinkling is the operative rule.

takeaway.text: Where a source text is silent about a transitional step, the Gemara reads that silence through governing principles already established elsewhere, here continuity of blood-service custody, rather than treating the silence as permission to deviate.

learning.ahaMoment: Blood that stays in one priest's hands from collection through sprinkling has unbroken chain of custody; the service's integrity, not the mishna's wording alone, is what drives the single-handler reading.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may wonder what happens if the slaughterer collects the blood by mistake, whether it could simply be handed to the designated sprinkler. The sugya does not address this contingency; its proofs establish the standard procedure, that the sprinkler collects, not the consequences of a deviation from it.
learning.memoryAnchor: The blood collector equals the blood sprinkler: one priest, unbroken custody, from neck to altar corner.

argumentFlow.label: Who collects the blood?
argumentFlow.text: The mishna assigns blood-sprinkling to a specific lottery winner but does not specify who collects the blood after slaughter.

argumentFlow.label: Sprinkler also collects
argumentFlow.text: The sprinkler collects the blood, since continuity from collection to sprinkling prevents the blood from becoming invalid.

argumentFlow.label: Proof from Tamid mishna count
argumentFlow.text: If the slaughterer collected the blood, the Tamid mishna's priest count would be wrong - proving the sprinkler is the collector.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Acha's additional support
argumentFlow.text: Rav Acha son of Rava adds a further proof from the Tamid mishna's explicit sequence.

theological: integrity of the blood service from collection to altar
narrative: multiple tannaitic proofs converging on same ruling

misconceptions.misconception: The slaughterer naturally collects the blood since he is already standing at the animal.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara establishes it is the designated sprinkler, not the slaughterer, who must collect the blood.

misconceptions.misconception: Blood collection and sprinkling are separate roles that can go to different priests.
misconceptions.correction: They must be performed by the same priest, to maintain unbroken continuity of the blood service.

visualizableElements.label: Blood service chain
visualizableElements.description: One priest: collects blood from neck, carries to altar, sprinkles on corners

visualizableElements.label: Slaughterer vs blood handler
visualizableElements.description: Two distinct roles: slaughterer ends his service at the neck; the sprinkler begins at the same moment

quizSeeds.question: Who collects the blood in the tamid service - the slaughterer or the sprinkler?
quizSeeds.answer: The sprinkler, who collects the blood and then sprinkles it himself to maintain continuity of the blood service.

quizSeeds.question: What is the practical concern behind having one priest do both blood collection and sprinkling?
quizSeeds.answer: To prevent the blood from becoming invalid through delay or improper transfer between priests.

sugyot.finalRuling: The priest who sprinkles the blood on the altar is the same priest who collected it after slaughter.

display.whats: Ben Azzai disputes the mishna's order of carrying tamid limbs; Rabbi Akiva explains they go in cutting order; Rava proves all tannaim agree the head goes first. The right leg always accompanies the head.
display.title: A three-way dispute that turns out to be one agreement: Rava on why the head always leads
display.hint: The sequence of limbs to the altar reflects both anatomical logic and the principle that the most significant limb (the head) leads.

learning.learnerQuestion: If Ben Azzai, the mishna, and R. Yosei HaGelili each give a different account of the tamid's limb order, are they really disagreeing with each other?
learning.coreTension: Three named traditions describing the same procedure look like a genuine three-way dispute over Temple practice, the kind that would normally require picking a winner.
learning.coreMove: Rava reframes the dispute by isolating what all three actually share: the head, accompanied by the right leg as the side of honor, is carried to the altar first in every version. What looked like competing full orderings collapses into one shared starting principle, with real disagreement confined to the lower-stakes question of what follows the head.
learning.resolution: All tannaim agree the head, together with the right leg, is carried to the altar first. The dispute among them is limited to the sequence of the remaining limbs.

takeaway.text: A dispute that looks total at first can collapse into agreement on the operative principle once a later authority isolates what the disputants actually share.

learning.ahaMoment: Rava's reconciliation shows that what looks like a fundamental dispute is actually agreement on the core principle, the head always leads, with disagreement only on the details of what follows.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the limb order carries the same stakes as the slaughter/sprinkling question, where the Gemara works hard to pin down exactly who must perform which role. This sugya does not state whether deviating from the limb order invalidates the offering or is merely a procedural preference; that question is left open here.
learning.memoryAnchor: All tannaim: head first, right leg alongside, like a general leading a march. Real dispute only over what follows.

argumentFlow.label: Ben Azzai's alternate limb order
argumentFlow.text: Ben Azzai, before Rabbi Akiva, transmits a different sequence for carrying the tamid's limbs from Rabbi Yehoshua.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Akiva: cutting order
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Akiva: the limbs go up in the order they were cut during the skinning, beginning with the head.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yosei HaGelili: head is the best piece
argumentFlow.text: R. Yosei HaGelili: 'every good piece' (Leviticus) means the head must go first as the most significant limb.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: all agree head goes first
argumentFlow.text: Rava reconciles: all tannaim agree the head goes first; they differ only on what limbs follow.

argumentFlow.label: Why right leg with the head?
argumentFlow.text: The right side is always more significant - the right leg accompanies the head as a mark of honor.

theological: 'every good piece' - honoring the divine service with the best first
narrative: Ben Azzai's transmission from R. Yehoshua

misconceptions.misconception: The tannaim fundamentally disagree about which limb goes to the altar first.
misconceptions.correction: Rava shows they all agree the head goes first; the dispute is confined to the order of the limbs that follow.

misconceptions.misconception: The right leg accompanies the head arbitrarily.
misconceptions.correction: It accompanies the head because the right side always takes precedence as the side of honor in Temple service.

visualizableElements.label: Tamid limb carrying order
visualizableElements.description: Sequence from head+right leg through the remaining limbs to the altar

visualizableElements.label: Mishna vs Ben Azzai vs R. Akiva
visualizableElements.description: Three traditions for the order of limbs, with Rava showing they converge on the head-first principle

quizSeeds.question: How does Rava reconcile the dispute between the mishna and Ben Azzai about the tamid limb order?
quizSeeds.answer: All tannaim agree the head goes to the altar first; they disagree only on the order of the limbs that follow.

quizSeeds.question: Why does the right leg accompany the head when the limbs are carried to the altar?
quizSeeds.answer: The right side takes precedence in Temple service - the right leg accompanies the most honorable limb (the head).

sugyot.finalRuling: The head goes first to the altar accompanied by the right front leg; the subsequent order is a tannaitic dispute.
